definition: Application-to-Person messaging (as opposed to person-to-person messaging like when you’re messaging your friends)
A tooth (or implant) that supports a dental prothesis (fake tooth)
Policies and technical measures that allow only authorized people or software to access ePHI.
A record of user or system activity used to monitor access and investigate incidents.
A review of which users currently have system access and whether that access is still appropriate.
A record of certain disclosures of PHI made by a covered entity.
Money owed to the practice by patients or insurers for services already provided.

A modification to the Patient’s balance which is not itself considered a Charge or a Payment.  Examples of positive (balance increasing) adjustments might be late fees, processing fees, etc.  Examples of negative (balance decreasing) adjustments might be good patient discounts, or (in some EHRs) write-offs.
Policies, procedures, and administrative actions used to manage security measures for ePHI.
The number of enabled sync agents for the data source.
The maximum amount a dental insurance plan will pay in a benefit year.
Application Programming Interface. A software interface that allows systems to exchange data programmatically.
Athena term for the waitlist of patients who want an earlier appointment.
Term used to refer to upper or lower denture
A Linux distro.
Hot-list designation for patients who want to come in sooner. Used in Dentrix, Dentrix Ascend, Dentrix Enterprise, Open Dental, and Athena.
Medical, cloud-based EHR that uses the Chrome extension exclusively.
In ModMed, the patient chart area where demographic information, medical history, alerts, and insurance-related information are referenced.
Hardware, software, or procedures that record and examine activity in systems containing ePHI.
A record showing what actions were taken, when, and by whom in a system or workflow.
A written permission from the individual allowing use or disclosure of PHI for certain purposes not otherwise permitted by HIPAA.
A security feature that ends an electronic session after a period of inactivity.
Amazon Web Services
Business Associate. A person or organization that performs services or functions for a covered entity and involves PHI.
The Ruby on Rails app that NexHealth hosts. It serves our SaaS app, and it is where the sync agents send the data to.
Business as Usual. Normal day-to-day operating work as opposed to special projects or escalations.
Periodic check-ins from our agents to report that they’re running, and any recent errors they’ve encountered.
A summary of what an insurance plan covers, including frequencies, percentages, maximums, and exclusions.
Tools menu used to map provider hours and appointment types for Denticon and Open Dental schedule blocks.
Generally, an impermissible use or disclosure that compromises the security or privacy of PHI.
The HIPAA rule that requires notifications after certain breaches of unsecured PHI.
A contract that defines how a business associate may use or disclose PHI and what safeguards are required.
The percentage of appointments canceled before they occur.
Transactions that happen via our email / text to pay capability. They are more risky and thus more expensive
Transactions that happen in the office at the terminal. These have lower rates because they are less risky and thus less expensive
The percentage of additional treatment plans presented to a patient that are accepted.
Community Dental Partners (is now called National Dental Partners)
Current Dental Terminology code set used to document and bill dental procedures.
Chrome Extension. A tool used by the cloud team to capture info from cloud EHR customers. Not to be confused with Halo: Combat Evolved.
The amount of clinical time scheduled or used for patient care.
The abstract concept of something for which a Patient is charged (most often a Procedure)
The current stage of an insurance claim, such as submitted, pending, paid, or denied.
Tools menu used to add a Google Tag and a redirect URL.
In Athena, the area where synced documents go.
Cloud-based orthodontic EHR that uses the Chrome extension exclusively.
Procedures that have already been performed and recorded in the chart.
A patient’s right to request communications by alternative means or at alternative locations.
A patient’s general agreement. Under HIPAA, consent is different from authorization.
A form used to record the patient’s agreement to treatment, communications, privacy practices, or other actions.
In Orthotrac, NexHealth reads Continuing Care appointments rather than a traditional waitlist.
Tools menu used to deploy custom Continuing Care Recall templates.
A health plan, healthcare clearinghouse, or healthcare provider directly subject to HIPAA.
Current Procedural Terminology code used for many medical services and procedures.
Customer Support
Customer Satisfaction Score
Customer Support Engineer
Customer Support Representative
The process of matching one system’s fields to another system’s fields during an integration.
How long information is kept according to policy or legal requirements.
Health-related data stripped of identifiers so it is no longer considered PHI under HIPAA standards.
The amount a patient must pay before insurance begins paying for covered services, depending on the plan.
An insurer decision not to pay for a submitted claim or service.
Dental, cloud-based EHR owned by Planet DDS.
Dental, on-prem/server-based EHR and one of the "Big 3" dental integrations.
Dental, cloud-based EHR (often abbreviated DxA) that uses the Chrome extension and is distinct from standard Dentrix.
Dental, on-prem/server-based EHR (often abbreviated DxE) used with multi-location enterprise customers.
A group of records maintained by or for a covered entity that is used to make decisions about individuals.
The release, transfer, or provision of access to PHI outside the organization holding it.
Decision Maker. Often the doctor or dentist.
Data Networks. Previous name of the Sync engineering team. Team builds and maintains the synchronization agents that allow data to flow to and from our customers’ systems
Folder/location where scanned or synced documents go in Dentrix, Dentrix Enterprise, and Denticon.
In Dentrix Ascend, the location where scanned documents go. Open Dental customers may also refer to Imaging as the document manager.
Practice-specific folders that completed forms sync into after a patient completes them; every NexHealth form has a designated document type.
In Cloud 9, the area where synced documents go.
Dental, on-prem/server-based EHR.
Dental Practice Management System (see EHR)
Medical, cloud-based EHR. The missed-appointment term is "no show."
Dental Service Organization. Multiple location institution. Often large, 10-30 offices. Has full executive team, marketing, etc.
Dentrix Ascend. A cloud version of Dentrix. Please never refer to this as dentrix because it can be confused with regular on-prem Dentrix and Dentrix Enterprise

Dentrix Enterprise. A multi-location EHR that shares a similar UI with the — typically single location — Dentrix. When cutting tickets for DXE, please use dentrix enterprise or dxe and not just dentrix''
Dental, on-prem/server-based EHR and one of the "Big 3" dental integrations.
eClinicalWorks. Cloud-based Practice Management System
Electronic Health Record. Used interchangeably with EMR and PMS. Engineering typically uses EHR instead of PMS or EMR.
Verification of whether a patient has active insurance coverage and applicable benefits.
A required procedure for obtaining needed ePHI during an emergency.
Many systems assume insurance is only offered through a guarantor’s employer, and thus there will be a record of what company that person works for.
Electronic Medical Record. Sometimes used synonymously with EHR and PMS.
Converting data into a protected form and restoring it to readable form for authorized use.
Protection of stored data through encryption.
Protection of data while it is being transmitted electronically.
A dental specialist focused on the study and treatment of the dental pulp (the inner layers of the tooth), often associated with root-canal treatment.
NexHealth forms app used with desktop and iPad workflows for forms and treatment-plan handoff.
Electronic Protected Health Information. PHI that is created, stored, received, or transmitted electronically.
Electronic Remittance Advice. The electronic version of insurer payment and adjudication details.
The process of routing a case or issue to a more specialized team or higher support level.
The process or act of removing a tooth.
eCW term for locations.
In Dentrix and Dentrix Enterprise, the patient chart holding demographic information, medical history, alerts, and insurance information.
Open Dental term used to refer to a patient's file.
A scheduling metric reflecting how successfully open appointment slots are filled.
Phone system used by CSRs and other teams; call recordings are also accessed there.
Family Messaging
Medical term for a post-appointment next step or visit.
In Curve, the location where synced documents go.
Tools menu used to generate Medical History forms and resync Document Types.
Federally Qualified Healthcare Center
A rule that restricts how often a service is covered, such as cleanings every six months.
Gross Processing Volume. The $ that the office processes with NexHealth each month

The person responsible for paying the Patient’s bills (in adult practice commonly the patient themselves, but with families may be a different person)  In examples below we’ll refer to one named “Mary Head-of-Household”
The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996, a U.S. law that sets standards for protecting certain health information.
A general phrase used to describe products, services, or organizations that support HIPAA requirements.
Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act. A U.S. law that strengthened HIPAA enforcement and breach notification requirements.
A preventive dental appointment, often involving cleaning, exam support, and periodontal review.
International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision. Diagnostic coding system used in medical documentation and billing.
Individually Identifiable Health Information. Health information that identifies, or could reasonably identify, a specific person.
In Open Dental, the location where synced documents go; practices may also use this term for the document manager.
A use or disclosure of PHI that is not allowed by the HIPAA Privacy Rule.
The process of identifying, investigating, containing, and addressing a security incident or breach.

Institution. The medical entity that is the parent of multiple practice locations. Small institutions could have one location. Larger ones could have dozens or hundreds of locations. Called an “Organization” within Enlive.
In NexHealth Tools, each account is considered an institution and can contain multiple locations.
An insurance company such as Aetna, Blue Cross, Cigna, or UnitedHealthcare.

A request for a given Insurance Subscription to pay for one or more Charges - often associated with estimates for how much will be paid per Charge, and sometimes write-offs for the uncovered amount per Charge

The association between an Insurance Claim and one of the Charges that it is intended to cover.  Our model for this includes estimate and Write-off information.  E.g. “We estimate that claim 123 will pay $25 of the $50 owed for charge 456, and that the write-off will be $25”.  This is a many-to-many relation - a given Charge may have multiple Insurance Claims, and a given Insurance Claim may cover multiple Charges.
The organization offering an Insurance Plan, e.g. “Kaiser”, “Aetna”, etc.

The union of a patient with an Insurance Subscription - e.g. “Timmy Son-of-Mary is covered under Mary’s subscription to the Kaiser Gold 500 plan” - not currently used in our payment models at the moment

The representation of an offering from a given Insurance Company at a Practice - might have many Insurance Subscriptions or only one, depending on how specific it is.  e.g. “Kaiser Gold 500 Plan”  Insurance Plans usually have a relation to an Employer also - e.g. there might be two entries named “Kaiser Gold 500 Plan” in the list of Insurance Plans: one offered by Employer A and one offered by Employer B.
The union of an Insurance Plan with a given Guarantor - e.g. “Mary Head-of-Household is subscribed to the Kaiser Gold 500 plan at employer SomeCo”
A patient-facing form used to collect demographics, insurance, consent, and health information before a visit.
Protecting ePHI from improper alteration or destruction.
A documented problem that has already been identified and may already have a workaround or planned fix.
Lead Development Representative
A security principle under which users are given the lowest level of access needed to perform their duties.

Financial terminology for a list of changes to the balance (amount owed) for someone or something. In our case, it’s keeping track of how much a given patient (or their guarantor) owes the Practice. We collectively refer to reading Payments, Adjustments, and Charges as “reading the ledger”
PHI with certain direct identifiers removed that may be used or disclosed for limited purposes under HIPAA.
Location. Place of business for a medical or dental practice. Larger institutions can have multiple locations or practices. Also see practice.
In NexHealth Tools, a subdivision within an institution; settings can be adjusted at the institution or location level.
A conclusion that PHI was likely not compromised after a risk assessment.
A NexHealth form generated from EHR data that can write back medical conditions, allergies, and medications into associated EHR fields.
Tools menu used to resubmit SMS registration information when needed.
A HIPAA standard requiring reasonable efforts to limit PHI access, use, or disclosure to the minimum needed for the intended purpose.
Steps taken to reduce harmful effects of an improper use, disclosure, or incident involving PHI.
Specialty medical, cloud-based EHR that does not use the Chrome extension.
Marketing Qualified Lead
Medical Service Organization. Medical equivalent of a DSO.
A customer setup with multiple practice locations under one organization.
North American Dental Group. A large Dental Service Organization customer.
An application that runs on a target operating system. Think of slack.exe or old video games like Red Alert 2. It is a program you download and install. May or may not connect to an external service.
Non-Decision Maker, but still important for adoption of NexHealth. Often the Office or Front Desk Manager.
National Dental Partners. Formerly the CDP (Community Dental Partners)
Access limited to the people who need the information to do their job.
This is represented by the fee that we charge for the transactions. We are assuming a rate of 2.5% (between card and card not present transactions to be conservative)
Group of medical providers contracted with a specific insurance company for the highest payment levels.
A patient’s first appointment with a practice, often involving registration, history, exam, and intake paperwork.
Medical, cloud-based EHR that does not use the Chrome extension.
DrChrono term for a missed appointment.
The percentage of appointments where patients fail to attend.
A document explaining how PHI may be used and disclosed and what rights patients have.
National Provider Identifier. A unique 10-digit number assigned to healthcare providers in the U.S.
Need to Know Friday. Our internal weekly broadcast about key product changes and new features to know about!
Online Booking
Office for Civil Rights. The HHS office that enforces HIPAA.
Office Manager
Sometimes shortened to OD. A mostly open-source EHR that is usually single location, but supports multi-location and enterprise customers.

Commonly shortened to op in code. Exam room or chair that the doctor or hygienist works in. May also refer to the column that the office manager uses to schedule a doctor/hygienist in the PMS. Some customers will assign create an op per provider, but that is rare.
A dental specialist whose focus is the treatment of malocclusion of the teeth and surrounding structures.
Orthodontics-focused, on-prem/server-based EHR.
The unpaid amount still owed on a patient account.
A collection of forms.
The larger entity that owns or manages one or more practice locations.
Someone receiving medical or dental care
In Cloud 9, the area where medical allergies, conditions, and medications sync.
In eCW, the location where synced documents/forms upload.
In Dentrix Ascend, the patient chart holding demographic information, medical history, alerts, and insurance information.
Medical term often used instead of forms.
In Eaglesoft, the patient chart holding demographic information, medical history, alerts, and insurance information.
The amount the patient owes after insurance processing, discounts, or adjustments.

A sum of money allocated to paying down the patient’s balance - may be a Direct Payment if it’s e.g. the Patient writing a check, or an Insurance Payment if it’s the result of an Insurance Claim. Some EHRs refer to our use of the term Payment as a “Pay Split” (i.e. if a Patient’s credit card is charged $100 to pay for a $75 Charge and a $25 Charge, we record that as two Payments, but some systems would call that one payment with two pay splits)
A product we offer to collect payments.

PDFWebServer. The Django app that runs Enlive. In production, this runs at [forms.nexhealth.com](http://forms.nexhealth.com/). It is mostly deprecated and only remains to support some legacy workflows.
Shortened term for more intensive gum cleaning or periodontal maintenance, often recommended every 3-4 months based on patient need.
Pertaining to the supporting and surrounding tissues or gums of the teeth.
More in-depth cleaning around the gum area for patients with periodontal disease or elevated risk; often referred to as "Perio."
Verifying that a person or system seeking access is who or what it claims to be.
Protected Health Information. Individually identifiable health information protected under HIPAA.
Measures used to protect buildings, workstations, devices, and systems containing ePHI.
Personally Identifiable Information. A superset of PHI
Practice Management System. Software medical & dental practices use to run their practice. Sometimes used synonymously with EMR and EHR.
Single location or few locations (<5). Also referred to as a location.
Medical term used as the equivalent of an office manager.
Insurer review or approval required before certain treatments or services are performed.
A request sent to insurance before treatment to estimate coverage, often used in dental workflows.
The HIPAA rule that sets national standards for the use and disclosure of PHI and establishes patient rights.

A medical or dental service which is provided to (usually “performed on”) a patient, for which there is usually a fee. In the NexHealth model, Procedure refers to the specific event that occurred (time, date, Procedure Code, etc)
The medical or dental code which specifies the type of procedure which was performed; can vary by practice, e.g. “D001” might refer to a given type of procedure

Dollar value of an appointment. Keep in mind that dental practices are essentially businesses and the practice is interested in high production appointments (e.g. a root canal or extraction) that would bring in more money.
Where our server software that can be publicly accessed by our customers and their patients. Thus if a software fix/change/feature is “in production” that means customers/patients can access it.
In eCW, the patient chart view holding demographic information, medical history, alerts, and insurance information; it must have a future appointment to sync.
Procedure code used in dental PMS, short for “prophylaxis”. What you would typically think of as a regular cleaning.
Treatment that has been recommended but not yet completed.
A doctor, nurse, hygienist, or someone else that provides care to a patient. Also refers to who bills the insurance.
A scheduling framework that defines how provider time is organized across days, hours, or appointment types.
Patient Experience. The team formerly known as SaaS.
A backlog of tasks, messages, or sync jobs waiting to be processed.
Root Cause Analysis. Investigation into the underlying cause of a problem or incident.
Manually entering or transferring data back into an EHR system.
A reminder scheduled for some period of time after a previous appointment prompting the patient to come back in.
The expected time between routine follow-up visits, such as six months or three months.
Medical term used as the equivalent of recare/recall.
Cavities (fillings)
A request by the patient to limit certain uses or disclosures of PHI.
A repeated attempt to complete a failed sync, writeback, or related system action.
A patient who has previously been seen by the practice.
Revenue Operations workflow used for requests such as account name changes, account hierarchy/location corrections, or updating the EHR on file.
A patient’s right to inspect or obtain a copy of PHI in a designated record set, with limited exceptions.
A patient’s right to request a correction or amendment to PHI in certain records.
An evaluation used to determine whether there is a low probability that PHI has been compromised.
Release of Information. The process or authorization used to disclose a patient’s records to another party.
Access granted according to a user’s job responsibilities.
Software as a Service. Us. We sell a software that is more of a service than a discreet product. This is a tech industry term.
A policy describing consequences for workforce members who fail to comply with privacy or security requirements.
Simply Beautiful Smiles
Tools menu used to set Book On minutes and Required minutes before booking.
SmileDirectClub. API partner
Sales Development Representative. Outbound Sales
Proper destruction or disposal of media or information so PHI cannot be reconstructed or recovered.
The attempted or successful unauthorized access, use, disclosure, modification, or destruction of information, or interference with system operations.
The HIPAA rule that sets national standards to protect ePHI.
A patient payment model in which the patient pays directly without insurance coverage.
A system setting that ends a session after inactivity.
Secure File Transfer Protocol. A secure method for moving files between systems.
The idea that both the platform/vendor and the customer share responsibility for protecting PHI.
Hot-list designation for patients who want a sooner appointment in Curve and Denticon.

Service Level Agreement. The amount of time (1 hour) we have to respond to clients as per their contracts. It is not actually written in their contracts, however NexHealth uses it as a metric to determine how quickly we are able to respond to customers' inquiries.
Reserved keywords that can be used to interpolate dynamic content into our campaigns and templates e.g. PATIENTNAME Some of these can also inject more interactive bits like buttons
Forms that are configured to be sent / shown conditionally based on many factors to automate exercising the right forms at the right time
In Eaglesoft, the location where scanned documents/forms are stored.
Small/Medium Business
Hot-list designation for patients who want a sooner appointment in Eaglesoft and Cloud 9.
Sales Qualified Leads
Scaling and Root Planing. Treatment for active gum disease that removes plaque and tartar from the tooth surface and below the gum line to clean infected gum pockets.
Single Sign On. When a third party manages your login services. e.g. we log in to NexHealth tools using our google accounts.
A vendor working for a business associate that may also create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI.
A failed read or write event between NexHealth and an external system.
Tools menu used to check sync read and write status.
Our Trademarked term for our integration with Health Record Systems
Taxpayer Identification Number used for practice or billing identification.
Technology and related policies used to protect ePHI and control access to it.
Usually used to refer to the NexHealth internal tools/configuration platform. See the tools section
Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations. Common categories of permitted PHI use and disclosure without separate authorization in many situations.
Measures that protect ePHI when it is sent over electronic networks.
A documented set of recommended procedures or next steps for a patient’s care.
The process of assessing and prioritizing incoming issues based on urgency, impact, or type.
A unique name or number assigned to identify and track a user’s identity.
The sharing, application, examination, or analysis of PHI within the organization that holds it.
The process of removing or disabling access when it is no longer needed.
The process of granting users appropriate system access.
Virtual Machine. A computer running in software instead of on hardware. We often have windows VMs housing our various EHR test environments.
General term for a list of patients who want an earlier appointment; some systems use EHR-specific alternatives like ASAP List, Appointment Wait List, or Short Notice List.
Web based application. There is not native installer or application and all interaction is through a web browser. Think Facebook. You don’t download facebook.exe, you just go to their website.
An automated event-based notification sent from one system to another when something happens.
A temporary method used to avoid or reduce the impact of an issue until a full fix is available.
Employees, volunteers, trainees, and other persons under the direct control of a covered entity or business associate.
eCW term for schedule blocks or blocks on the schedule.

A portion of the original fee on a Charge which the Practice will not be collecting, usually because it exceeds the amount in their contract with a given Insurance Plan for a given Procedure Code.  Depending on the EHR, these may be recorded as Adjustments or via Insurance Claim-Charges
